title: Linux Foundation Launches Akrites to Protect Critical Open Source Software from AI-Powered  Threats
gist: The Linux Foundation launched Akrites, a 20+ member coalition (AWS, Anthropic, Google, Microsoft, OpenAI, NVIDIA, Red Hat and others) building a shared incident-response and coordinated-disclosure layer for critical open source, premised on generative AI having collapsed the window between vulnerability discovery and exploitation.

insights.claim: Generative AI has fundamentally altered the attacker/defender balance in software security, letting AI models discover vulnerabilities in widely used software in minutes rather than weeks.
insights.speaker: Craig Risi
insights.so_what: The discovery-to-exploit window is compressing structurally, raising demand for automated defensive tooling and coordinated response vendors.
insights.quote: AI models can now discover vulnerabilities in widely used software in minutes rather than weeks
insights.locator: The initiative is rooted in a growing concern

insights.claim: Exploit code can now be generated almost immediately after security patches become public, leaving maintainers little time to respond before weaponization.
insights.speaker: Craig Risi
insights.so_what: Patch-then-exploit races favor attackers; watch for tooling that shortens deploy time post-disclosure.
insights.quote: exploit code can be generated almost immediately after security patches become public

insights.claim: Frontier AI models lower the barrier for attackers to automate vulnerability research at unprecedented scale, cutting both ways for offense and defense.
insights.speaker: Craig Risi
insights.so_what: Security spend and open-source funding become a structural growth area as AI-assisted attacks scale.
insights.locator: The launch reflects a broader shift

insights.claim: The Linux Foundation launched Akrites, backed by 20+ founding organizations, to coordinate vulnerability remediation across critical open source projects via a shared Security Incident Response Team and a standardized Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure process.
insights.speaker: Craig Risi
insights.so_what: A new industry body concentrating open-source security coordination — a hub worth tracking for downstream tooling and standards.
insights.locator: The Linux Foundation has launched Akrites

insights.claim: Akrites' founding members span cloud providers, AI labs, chipmakers and finance — including Amazon Web Services, Anthropic, Google, Microsoft, OpenAI, NVIDIA, IBM, Red Hat, Cisco, Chainguard, and Sonatype.
insights.speaker: Craig Risi
insights.so_what: Rare alignment of rival AI and cloud giants around one security body signals the threat is viewed as systemic, not competitive.
insights.locator: Founding members include Amazon Web Services

insights.claim: Rather than another security scanning tool, Akrites builds an operational response layer focused on how critical vulnerabilities are handled after discovery — private validation, coordinated patching, and synchronized disclosure.
insights.speaker: Craig Risi
insights.so_what: The value shift is from finding vulnerabilities to fixing and deploying them first; incumbents in scanning are not directly displaced.
insights.locator: Rather than creating another security scanning tool

insights.claim: Akrites complements rather than replaces OpenSSF and the Linux Foundation's Alpha-Omega program, adding a remediation-coordination layer on top of their standards-and-tooling focus.
insights.speaker: Craig Risi
insights.so_what: Maps the open-source security stack: standards (OpenSSF) vs operational response (Akrites) — useful for positioning security startups.
insights.locator: Akrites does not replace existing initiatives

insights.claim: The founding members are betting that collaborative, ecosystem-wide coordination can scale as quickly as AI itself, an approach whose sufficiency is still unproven.
insights.speaker: Craig Risi
insights.so_what: An open bet on collaboration-vs-AI-speed; failure would validate more automated/commercial defensive models.
insights.quote: betting that collaboration can scale as quickly as AI itself
